title: Shoulder Dystocia at Delivery with HELPERR Maneuvers + Erb's Palsy in Macrosomic Newborn
subtitle: Turtle Sign Recognition, HELPERR Sequence (H-E-L-P-E-R-R), McRoberts + Suprapubic Pressure (NOT Fundal — Worsens Impaction), Erb's Palsy Waiter's Tip Recognition, Documented Head-to-Body Time

patient.name: S. Castellanos
patient.admitDate: Yesterday, 2330 (L&D admission for spontaneous labor at 41+1 weeks)
patient.room: L&D Room 4 — delivery just completed at 1432 (head 1430, body 1432); now in immediate postpartum

vitals.time: 1445 (CURRENT — 13 min post-delivery; fundus firm, lochia moderate)

labs.v: G3P2002 at 41+1 weeks (postterm); GESTATIONAL DIABETES (A1c 6.2% — diet-controlled; not on insulin); estimated fetal weight by 38-wk growth US = 4400g (>90th percentile = MACROSOMIA); maternal BMI 32 (obesity); prior delivery (P2) macrosomic infant (4100g) without shoulder dystocia in 2022; height 5'2" (158 cm — short stature); excessive maternal weight gain this pregnancy (38 lb gained); pelvic shape: gynecoid (US assessment)
labs.r: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA RISK FACTORS: MACROSOMIA (>4000g but especially >4500g), MATERNAL DIABETES (3–7× risk via fetal hyperinsulinism + abnormal shoulder-to-head ratio), PRIOR shoulder dystocia (recurrence 10–25%), POSTTERM (>42 wk), prolonged 2nd stage labor, instrumental vaginal delivery (forceps/vacuum), maternal obesity, advanced maternal age. Most cases occur in patients WITHOUT identifiable risk factors — preparedness required for all deliveries

labs.v: Head delivered at 1430 over INTACT perineum after 2nd-stage labor of 90 min; immediately after head delivery, head RETRACTED BACK against perineum ('turtle sign' — head appears to draw back into birth canal like turtle into shell); failure of restitution + external rotation; failure of anterior shoulder to descend with maternal pushing effort + gentle downward traction — SHOULDER DYSTOCIA RECOGNIZED at 1430
labs.r: TURTLE SIGN = pathognomonic for shoulder dystocia. Anterior shoulder impacted behind PUBIC SYMPHYSIS; cannot deliver with routine downward traction. Recognition: failure of restitution, failure of anterior shoulder to deliver with gentle traction + maternal effort, turtle sign. TIME-SENSITIVE — every minute of head-to-body delay risks neonatal asphyxia, brachial plexus injury, fracture, death

labs.v: H — CALL FOR HELP at 1430: additional L&D nurse + OB attending (already at bedside) + 2nd OB + anesthesia + NICU notified to come to room (delivered preterm neonatologist alert); doula + father informed; time-of-onset noted by RN; E — EPISIOTOMY: considered but DEFERRED initially (does not relieve bony obstruction; creates room for internal maneuvers if needed later — mediolateral episiotomy performed at 1431 after McRoberts + suprapubic pressure insufficient); L — LEGS / McROBERTS MANEUVER: hyperflexion of maternal thighs onto abdomen (knees-to-chest position) — done by L&D nurses bilaterally; reduces pelvic inclination, flattens lumbar lordosis, opens pelvic outlet — successful in ~40% alone; P — SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE: nurse applied downward + lateral pressure just above pubic symphysis with palm (NOT fundal pressure — worsens impaction!) to dislodge anterior shoulder; E — ENTER (internal rotation maneuvers): OB inserted hand vaginally for RUBIN MANEUVER (push anterior shoulder toward fetal chest to dislodge from behind symphysis) then WOOD'S SCREW (rotate posterior shoulder 180° corkscrew); R — REMOVE posterior arm: OB swept posterior arm across fetal chest and delivered; R — ROLL (Gaskin all-fours maneuver): NOT NEEDED — body delivered after posterior arm delivery at 1432
labs.r: HELPERR MNEMONIC: H-Help, E-Episiotomy (consider; doesn't relieve bony obstruction), L-Legs (McRoberts ~40% success alone), P-suprapubic Pressure (NOT FUNDAL), E-Enter (Rubin, Wood's screw, reverse Wood's), R-Remove posterior arm, R-Roll (Gaskin all-fours). Each maneuver attempted ~30 sec before moving to next. Total time should be <5 min ideally. AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE — worsens impaction + can cause uterine rupture

labs.v: Head delivery: 1430:15; head-to-body interval: ~2 minutes (1430:15 → 1432:18); maneuvers performed (in order): call for help, McRoberts maneuver (1430:30), suprapubic pressure (1430:50), mediolateral episiotomy (1431:00), Rubin maneuver (1431:15), Wood's screw (1431:45), posterior arm delivery (1432:10); body delivered 1432:18; personnel present (named); fetal heart rate during dystocia (monitored continuously); maternal vital signs; estimated blood loss; baby resuscitation per NRP
labs.r: DOCUMENTATION of shoulder dystocia must include: TIME of head delivery, TIME of body delivery (head-to-body interval), ALL MANEUVERS performed in ORDER with TIMES, PERSONS present (named), estimated FORCE applied, FETAL HEART RATE pattern, MATERNAL VITAL SIGNS, ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS, NEONATAL ASSESSMENT including Apgars + presence/absence of brachial plexus injury or fracture + initial resuscitation. Required for quality assessment + medico-legal protection

labs.v: BIRTH: male, weight 4520g (large for gestational age — macrosomic; just above 4500g threshold = severe macrosomia); APGAR 4 at 1 minute (HR 88, weak grimace, blue-pink, weak respiratory effort, weak tone), APGAR 8 at 5 minutes (HR 142, strong cry, pink, active tone) after NRP resuscitation (PPV with bag-mask 30 sec, then crying with stimulation, transitioned to CPAP brief, then air); cord pH 7.18 (mildly acidotic — moderate stress); cord lactate 4.2; NICU notified + bedside; transitioned to mother for skin-to-skin once stable; admitted to mother-baby unit observation status
labs.r: Macrosomic infants ≥4500g have HIGHER rates of shoulder dystocia (~10–25%); when shoulder dystocia occurs, neonatal complications include: BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY (1–20% — see ERB'S PALSY below), FRACTURE clavicle (most common; usually heals well) or humerus (rare), HIE (hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy from prolonged hypoxia), ASPHYXIA, death (rare). NRP resuscitation per standard protocol; cord ABG documents acid-base status; NICU evaluation

labs.v: On neonatal exam at 1445: RIGHT ARM with CLASSIC ERB'S PALSY presentation — arm ADDUCTED, INTERNALLY ROTATED, ELBOW EXTENDED, WRIST FLEXED (the 'WAITER'S TIP' position); ABSENT MORO REFLEX on right (asymmetric Moro); intact grasp reflex (suggests C8–T1 spared); intact biceps reflex slightly diminished on right; passive ROM normal (rules out fracture clavicle/humerus by exam); X-ray pending to rule out fracture (especially clavicle); orthopedic + neurology consultation; gentle passive ROM only initially (no aggressive movement); plan immobilization in functional position
labs.r: ERB'S PALSY = upper brachial plexus injury (C5–C6) from excessive lateral neck traction during shoulder dystocia delivery; classic 'WAITER'S TIP' position (arm adducted, internally rotated, elbow extended, wrist flexed). PROGNOSIS: 80–90% spontaneous recovery within first 3–6 months with conservative management (gentle ROM, immobilization in functional position, PT); persistent deficit at 3–6 months → microsurgical nerve repair consideration. KLUMPKE'S PALSY (less common) = lower brachial plexus injury C8–T1 with 'CLAW HAND' + Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhydrosis if T1 sympathetic chain involved)

labs.n: AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE — the critical rule
labs.v: Fundal pressure during shoulder dystocia is CONTRAINDICATED — it WORSENS the impaction by pushing the anterior shoulder MORE FIRMLY against the pubic symphysis, can cause UTERINE RUPTURE, can worsen brachial plexus stretch injury; SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (palm above pubic symphysis with downward + lateral pressure) is the correct technique — dislodges anterior shoulder forward + laterally from behind symphysis
labs.r: FUNDAL VS SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE distinction is high-yield. FUNDAL pressure = forbidden during shoulder dystocia. SUPRAPUBIC pressure = part of HELPERR sequence (the second P). The names sound similar but locations + effects are opposite. All L&D staff trained in distinction; documented if anyone applies fundal pressure inadvertently (rare but error to recognize)

labs.v: Immediate postpartum 1445: PERINEUM: mediolateral episiotomy + 2nd-degree extension repaired; small periurethral laceration repaired; no evidence of 3rd or 4th-degree tear; cervical inspection: small posterior cervical tear noted + sutured; FUNDUS: firm, midline, at umbilicus; LOCHIA: moderate rubra (no excessive bleeding); ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: 450 mL (normal range); PLACENTA: delivered intact at 1440; no signs of uterine rupture (uterus firm, no tachycardia/hypotension); patient comfortable, talking with father, holding baby skin-to-skin
labs.r: MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS of shoulder dystocia: POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (PPH) from uterine atony (overdistended uterus from macrosomic infant; multiple maneuvers can cause atony) or trauma (3rd/4th-degree perineal tears, cervical tears, vaginal sulcus tears); UTERINE RUPTURE (rare; risk with fundal pressure); BLADDER/URETHRAL trauma; PERIPARTUM PSYCHOLOGICAL TRAUMA (witnessing emergent delivery)

labs.v: Gestational diabetes diagnosed at 26 weeks (1-hr GTT 168, 3-hr GTT failed); diet-controlled (HbA1c 6.2% — adequate); fasting BG 78–95, postprandial <140 (in target); insulin not required; baby BG on admission to nursery 38 mg/dL (low — typical for IDM/IGDM; needs immediate feeding + recheck q30 min initially)
labs.r: INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) / Infant of Gestational Diabetic (IGDM): risk for HYPOGLYCEMIA (fetal hyperinsulinism from maternal hyperglycemia; abrupt cessation of glucose supply at delivery → reactive hypoglycemia), respiratory distress (delayed lung maturity), polycythemia, hypocalcemia, hyperbilirubinemia, congenital anomalies (pre-existing DM more than GDM). Treat hypoglycemia: feed (breast or formula); BG q30 min initially; D10W IV if BG <40 OR symptomatic

labs.v: 1445: HR 96, BP 128/78, RR 18, SpO2 98%, T 98.6°F; fundus firm midline at umbilicus; lochia moderate rubra; perineum (episiotomy repair intact, mild edema, no expanding hematoma); pain 4/10 with ibuprofen 600 mg PO given; bonding well with infant (skin-to-skin); voided 200 mL after foley removed; ambulating to bathroom with assist; husband supportive at bedside
labs.r: Routine immediate postpartum assessment: fundus firmness + position; lochia amount + color; perineum (episiotomy, lacerations, hematoma); vital signs (PPH watch); pain (multimodal: ibuprofen + acetaminophen, low-dose opioid PRN); bladder (urinary retention common after epidural + delivery); bonding + breastfeeding initiation; family support

labs.v: Apgar 4/8 (1/5 min); birth weight 4520 g (severe macrosomia); length 53 cm; head circumference 36 cm; CLASSIC ERB'S PALSY R arm (waiter's tip position, absent Moro on R, intact grasp); CLAVICLE: palpable crepitus on R clavicle = suspected fracture (X-ray confirms — clavicle fracture, common with macrosomia + shoulder dystocia, usually heals without intervention); BG 38 (hypoglycemia from IDM physiology; fed breastmilk + recheck q30 min); good urine output; mec in delivery (was suctioned per NRP); no facial bruising or palsy; cord ABG pH 7.18 (mild acidemia, recovered by 5 min); NICU evaluation deferred (infant stable, transitioning well; will room with mother)
labs.r: MACROSOMIC NEONATAL COMPLICATIONS: BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY (Erb's or Klumpke's), CLAVICLE FRACTURE (most common — usually palpable crepitus, X-ray for confirmation, heals well with immobilization in functional position), HUMERUS FRACTURE (less common), HIE/asphyxia (if prolonged), HYPOGLYCEMIA (IDM), POLYCYTHEMIA, HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA. Most clavicle fractures heal completely without specific intervention beyond gentle handling + immobilization

labs.v: Cord ABG: pH 7.18 (mild acidemia), pCO2 58, HCO3 24, BE −5; cord lactate 4.2; infant BG 38 (hypoglycemia); CBC: Hgb 19.8 (high — IDM polycythemia tendency), Plt 248; bilirubin baseline (will monitor for hyperbilirubinemia); newborn screen pending; maternal BMP normal; maternal CBC: Hgb 11.4 (down from 12.0 preop — expected), Plt 198
labs.r: Cord ABG quantifies degree of intrapartum stress — pH <7.0 = severe acidemia + concern for HIE; pH 7.0–7.1 = significant stress; pH >7.2 = generally reassuring. Infant BG monitoring per IDM/IGDM protocol. Polycythemia (Hct >65%) considered with high Hgb; rarely requires partial exchange transfusion (only if symptomatic + Hct >65%)

labs.v: Mother: routine immediate postpartum care; pain management; lactation support; bonding + skin-to-skin; ambulation as tolerated; observe for PPH (high risk); discharge in 24–48 hr if uneventful. Baby: rooming with mother; X-ray R clavicle confirms fracture (no specific treatment beyond gentle handling); ORTHOPEDIC + NEUROLOGY consultation for Erb's palsy (immobilize in functional position; gentle passive ROM only; conservative management 3–6 mo with PT; reassess for nerve repair if persistent deficit); LACTATION support for IDM (frequent feeding to prevent recurrent hypoglycemia); BG q30 min until stable >40, then q3–6h x 24 hr; bilirubin monitoring; discharge with PT referral + orthopedic follow-up + ID follow-up if any concerns
labs.r: Continued care plan addresses all identified issues. Multidisciplinary coordination (pediatrics, orthopedics, neurology, PT/OT, lactation, social work). Parental education on Erb's palsy course + expectations (most resolve in 3–6 months), positioning + ROM exercises, when to be concerned (no improvement at 1 month → urgent evaluation)

labs.v: Recurrence risk for shoulder dystocia ~10–25% (increased if same or higher fetal weight, continued GDM, postterm); pre-pregnancy weight optimization (BMI), DIABETES SCREENING + management (HbA1c <6.5% pre-pregnancy and throughout); planned induction at 39–40 weeks if next infant predicted macrosomic >4500g; ELECTIVE CESAREAN may be considered for estimated fetal weight >5000g (no diabetes) OR >4500g (with diabetes) per ACOG; pregnancy planning discussion at 6-week postpartum visit
labs.r: Future pregnancy planning. ACOG considers cesarean for predicted fetal weight thresholds. Diabetes control + weight optimization reduce recurrence. Shared decision-making with patient. Document in chart for future provider reference

labs.v: FATHER at bedside throughout, supportive; helped with maternal counter-pressure during pushing + remained calm during emergency; will likely experience post-event anxiety (witnessing emergent delivery); SIBLING (4 yr old) at grandmother's; chaplain available; social work + lactation consult; lactation support emphasized for IDM hypoglycemia prevention via frequent feeding; postpartum depression screening at 2 + 6 week visits
labs.r: Family-centered care + psychological support. Witnessing emergency delivery can cause PTSD-like symptoms in partners; offer support resources. Postpartum depression screening per ACOG (Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale at 2 + 6 wk visits); higher risk after birth trauma; provide mental health resources

nursesNote: 1445 — 33 y/o G3P2002 at 41+1 weeks with GDM (diet-controlled, A1c 6.2%), macrosomic estimated fetal weight 4400g, prior 4100g infant delivered without complication, BMI 32, short stature 5'2", admitted yesterday 2330 in spontaneous labor. Epidural for analgesia. Progressed normally to full dilation; pushed 90 min in 2nd stage; HEAD DELIVERED at 1430:15 over INTACT perineum. IMMEDIATELY noted TURTLE SIGN — head retracted back against perineum + failure of restitution + failure of anterior shoulder to descend with maternal pushing + gentle downward traction = SHOULDER DYSTOCIA recognized. CALLED FOR HELP at 1430:18 (additional L&D nurse + already-bedside OB attending + 2nd OB + anesthesia + NICU notified). HELPERR sequence performed in standard order: H — CALL FOR HELP (done); E — EPISIOTOMY (mediolateral done at 1431:00 after McRoberts + suprapubic insufficient to create room for internal maneuvers — note: doesn't relieve bony obstruction but creates space); L — McROBERTS MANEUVER 1430:30 (L&D nurses bilateral knees-to-chest hyperflexion of maternal thighs onto abdomen — reduces pelvic inclination, flattens lumbar lordosis, opens outlet — successful ~40% alone); P — SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE 1430:50 (RN applied downward + lateral pressure ABOVE pubic symphysis with palm — to dislodge anterior shoulder; NOTED THAT FUNDAL PRESSURE IS CONTRAINDICATED — worsens impaction + can cause uterine rupture); E — ENTER (OB Rubin maneuver 1431:15 — push anterior shoulder toward fetal chest to dislodge from behind symphysis; Wood's screw 1431:45 — rotate posterior shoulder 180° corkscrew); R — REMOVE POSTERIOR ARM 1432:00 (OB swept posterior arm across fetal chest and delivered); BODY DELIVERED 1432:18 — total head-to-body interval ~2 MINUTES. Gaskin (R-Roll all-fours) maneuver NOT needed. NEONATAL OUTCOME: male, 4520g (severe macrosomia >4500g); APGAR 4 at 1 min (HR 88, weak grimace, blue-pink, weak respiratory effort, weak tone) → 8 at 5 min after NRP (PPV bag-mask 30 sec, then crying with stimulation, brief CPAP, then air); cord pH 7.18 (mild acidemia); cord lactate 4.2; NICU notified, evaluated bedside, deferred admission (stable, transitioning, rooming with mother). RIGHT ERB'S PALSY identified on neonatal exam: classic WAITER'S TIP position (arm adducted, internally rotated, elbow extended, wrist flexed); absent Moro on R; intact grasp (C8–T1 spared); passive ROM normal (rules out fracture by exam) but palpable CLAVICLE CREPITUS on R = X-ray confirms R CLAVICLE FRACTURE (common with macrosomia + shoulder dystocia; usually heals without intervention). Hypoglycemia BG 38 (IDM physiology — fetal hyperinsulinism from maternal hyperglycemia + abrupt cessation of glucose supply at delivery → reactive hypoglycemia) → fed breastmilk immediately + recheck BG q30 min; D10W IV ready if BG <40 OR symptomatic. ORTHOPEDIC + NEUROLOGY consultation for Erb's palsy + clavicle fracture: immobilize R arm in functional position pinned to clothes; gentle passive ROM only initially (no aggressive movement); 80–90% spontaneous recovery in first 3–6 months with conservative management + PT; reassess at 1 month — if no improvement, urgent neurology evaluation for nerve repair consideration. DOCUMENTATION (medico-legal critical): time of head delivery (1430:15), time of body delivery (1432:18), all maneuvers performed in order with times, persons present (named in delivery note), estimated force applied, fetal heart rate during dystocia, maternal vital signs, EBL 450 mL (normal), placenta delivered intact at 1440, NEONATAL ASSESSMENT including Apgars + Erb's palsy + clavicle fracture + initial resuscitation per NRP. MATERNAL POSTPARTUM 1445: fundus firm midline at umbilicus, lochia moderate rubra, mediolateral episiotomy + 2nd-degree extension + small periurethral laceration repaired, small posterior cervical tear sutured, EBL 450 mL (normal range), HR 96, BP 128/78, RR 18, SpO2 98%; ibuprofen 600 mg PO for pain; voided 200 mL after foley removed; bonding well with infant via skin-to-skin; husband supportive at bedside (helped with pushing + remained calm during emergency — postpartum will assess for partner PTSD-like symptoms common after emergency deliveries). DIABETES MANAGEMENT: continue diet; baby BG monitoring q30 min until stable >40 then q3–6h x 24 hr; frequent breastfeeding to prevent recurrent hypoglycemia. POSTPARTUM PPH RISK assessment: macrosomic infant + uterine overdistention + multiple maneuvers + 2nd stage prolonged = ELEVATED PPH risk; oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR running prophylactically; close monitoring of fundus + lochia + EBL + vital signs over next 24 hr. FUTURE PREGNANCY COUNSELING at 6-wk visit: recurrence risk 10–25%; pre-pregnancy weight optimization + diabetes control (HbA1c <6.5%); planned induction at 39–40 wk if next predicted macrosomic; elective cesarean for predicted fetal weight >5000g (no diabetes) OR >4500g (with diabetes) per ACOG. POSTPARTUM DEPRESSION + BIRTH TRAUMA screening planned at 2 + 6 wk visits (witnessing emergency delivery + Erb's palsy diagnosis in infant = elevated PPD/PTSD risk); mental health resources offered. Family-centered care + chaplain + social work + lactation consult + PT referral + orthopedic + neurology coordination. Anticipated mother discharge in 24–48 hr if uneventful; baby remains rooming with mother for monitoring + lactation support; pediatric + orthopedic follow-up within 1 week of discharge.
providerOrders: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA was managed per HELPERR algorithm at 1430–1432 (documented above); BODY DELIVERED 1432:18; total head-to-body interval ~2 minutes

FUTURE SHOULDER DYSTOCIA management reminder: HELPERR MNEMONIC — H (Help — call for help), E (Episiotomy — consider; doesn't relieve bony obstruction but creates room), L (Legs — McRoberts maneuver hyperflexion of thighs ~40% success alone), P (suprapubic Pressure — NOT FUNDAL; downward + lateral palm above symphysis), E (Enter — internal rotation maneuvers Rubin + Wood's screw + reverse Wood's), R (Remove posterior arm), R (Roll — Gaskin all-fours)
⚠️ AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE during shoulder dystocia — WORSENS impaction (pushes anterior shoulder more firmly against pubic symphysis), can cause UTERINE RUPTURE, worsens brachial plexus stretch injury

DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED for any shoulder dystocia: time of head delivery, time of body delivery (head-to-body interval), maneuvers performed IN ORDER with times, persons present, estimated force applied, fetal heart rate pattern, maternal vital signs, EBL, placenta status, neonatal assessment (Apgars + brachial plexus exam + fracture assessment + initial resuscitation)

MATERNAL POSTPARTUM CARE: routine assessment (fundus, lochia, perineum, VS) q15 min x 4 hr, q30 min x 2 hr, q1h until stable; PPH PROPHYLAXIS — OXYTOCIN 20 units in 1 L LR continuous (already running) for the elevated PPH risk (macrosomia + uterine overdistention + multiple maneuvers + prolonged 2nd stage); IBUPROFEN 600 mg PO q6h scheduled for pain + uterine cramping; ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg PO q6h alternating; low-dose oxycodone PRN severe pain; perineum ice + sitz baths after 24 hr; bladder management (Foley removed; void within 6 hr, recheck if not)

If PPH develops: BIMANUAL FUNDAL MASSAGE + uterotonic ladder (oxytocin already running → consider methergine 0.2 mg IM if NO HTN — patient does not have HTN so this is OK; or HEMABATE 250 mcg IM if no asthma; MISOPROSTOL 800–1000 mcg PR if HTN + asthma) + TXA 1 g IV within 3 hr of bleeding onset (WOMAN trial); MTP if EBL >1000 mL with hemodynamic compromise

NEONATAL CARE: Apgar 4/8 (1/5 min), 4520 g, NRP resuscitation completed; transitioning well; rooming with mother; pediatric assessment q4h; CORD ABG pH 7.18 (mild acidemia — clinically stable, monitor for HIE signs); CONTINUOUS pulse oximetry x 24 hr

ERB'S PALSY management: IMMOBILIZE R arm in functional position (sleeve pinned across chest, position changed q2h); GENTLE PASSIVE ROM only initially (no aggressive movement); ORTHOPEDIC + NEUROLOGY + PT/OT consultation; X-ray R upper extremity (clavicle confirmed fracture; humerus normal — no fracture); 80–90% spontaneous recovery in 3–6 months with conservative management; reassess at 1 month — if no improvement, urgent neurology referral for nerve repair consideration; parental education on positioning, ROM exercises, expectations, when to be concerned

CLAVICLE FRACTURE (confirmed on X-ray): no specific intervention beyond gentle handling + immobilization in functional position (often spontaneously heals with no treatment); pinning sleeve to clothes; reassess at 2 weeks (callus formation expected); usually heals completely without long-term effect

IDM HYPOGLYCEMIA management: BG q30 min until stable >40 mg/dL then q3–6h x 24 hr; FEED breastmilk frequently (q2–3h; consider supplementation with donor breastmilk or formula if maternal supply not yet established); D10W IV via PIV if BG <40 mg/dL OR symptomatic (jitteriness, lethargy, poor feeding, hypothermia, seizure); after 24 hr stable feeding + BG, can de-escalate monitoring
Lactation consult bedside; encourage skin-to-skin; demonstrate positioning for breastfeeding (modified football hold may be easier given Erb's palsy on R arm); pumping if supplementation needed

Bilirubin monitoring per AAP protocol — risk for hyperbilirubinemia in macrosomic IDM + clavicle fracture (cephalohematoma + clavicle fracture increase bilirubin from hemoglobin breakdown); transcutaneous bilirubin or serum bilirubin per nomogram

Hgb 19.8 (POLYCYTHEMIA tendency in IDM; observe for symptoms — plethora, lethargy, hypoglycemia recurrence, poor feeding, jitteriness, respiratory distress); partial exchange transfusion only if symptomatic + Hct >65% (rare)

If signs of HIE develop (encephalopathy, seizures, abnormal tone, abnormal feeding): NICU admission, neurology consultation, EEG, head US, possible THERAPEUTIC HYPOTHERMIA (cooling) within 6 hr of birth (cooling reduces mortality + neurodevelopmental disability in moderate-severe HIE)

FAMILY-CENTERED CARE: father at bedside; chaplain available; social work for psychosocial support; postpartum depression screening at 2 + 6 wk visits (higher PPD/PTSD risk after birth trauma + neonatal complications); partner support resources (witnessing emergency delivery is traumatic)

DISCHARGE PLANNING (mother): 24–48 hr if uneventful; lactation support continued at home + outpatient lactation consultant; postpartum 1-week + 6-week visits with screening; future pregnancy counseling at 6-week visit (recurrence risk, pre-pregnancy weight + diabetes optimization, planned delivery approach)

DISCHARGE PLANNING (baby): rooms with mother typically; pediatric follow-up within 1 week (BG, weight, feeding, bilirubin, Erb's palsy assessment); orthopedic follow-up at 2 weeks for clavicle fracture; neurology + PT at 2–4 weeks for Erb's palsy progress; ID neonatal screen + newborn metabolic screen complete

MEDICO-LEGAL DOCUMENTATION: comprehensive delivery note with all elements (above); incident report not required for textbook shoulder dystocia managed per protocol with reasonable outcome; if injury occurred (Erb's palsy here is unfortunately common), document course of care + informed parental communication; risk management notification per institutional policy

Postpartum quality improvement / debriefing: optional team debrief with everyone present at delivery to review timing + maneuvers + outcomes; identify any improvements; reinforces team training for future events; not punitive — culture of safety + learning

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: TURTLE SIGN at delivery: head retracted back against perineum + failure of restitution + failure of anterior shoulder to descend with gentle traction + maternal effort — SHOULDER DYSTOCIA recognized
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — obstetric emergency recognition. Turtle sign is pathognomonic. Time-sensitive — every minute of head-to-body delay risks neonatal asphyxia, brachial plexus injury, fracture, death. CALL FOR HELP immediately + initiate HELPERR sequence.

opts.text: HELPERR sequence performed in standard order (H-Help, E-Episiotomy, L-Legs/McRoberts, P-suprapubic Pressure, E-Enter internal maneuvers, R-Remove posterior arm, R-Roll Gaskin) — body delivered at 1432:18, head-to-body 2 min total
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — standardized management. Each maneuver ~30 sec before moving to next. Total <5 min ideally. Patient delivered in 2 min — efficient response. Documentation of times + maneuvers is critical for medico-legal protection.

opts.text: AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE — worsens impaction (pushes anterior shoulder more firmly against pubic symphysis), can cause uterine rupture, worsens brachial plexus stretch; use SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (palm above symphysis, downward + lateral) instead
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-yield safety distinction. Fundal vs suprapubic pressure — opposite effects. Suprapubic dislodges anterior shoulder. Fundal worsens impaction. Critical knowledge for ALL L&D nurses.

opts.text: RIGHT ERB'S PALSY recognition: classic WAITER'S TIP position (arm adducted, internally rotated, elbow extended, wrist flexed); absent Moro on R; intact grasp (C8–T1 spared) — brachial plexus injury C5–C6 from delivery traction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognized neonatal complication. Erb's palsy = upper brachial plexus (C5–C6) injury from lateral neck traction during dystocia. 80–90% spontaneous recovery in 3–6 months with conservative management. Klumpke's (lower brachial C8–T1 with claw hand + Horner's) less common.

opts.text: Right CLAVICLE FRACTURE confirmed on X-ray — palpable crepitus + pain on R clavicle; common with macrosomia + shoulder dystocia; usually heals without specific intervention beyond gentle handling + immobilization
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — common shoulder dystocia complication. Most common neonatal fracture. Distinguishes from Erb's palsy by passive ROM (intact in Erb's, painful in clavicle fracture). Pinning sleeve to clothes for immobilization. Heals well.

opts.text: MACROSOMIC newborn 4520 g (severe macrosomia >4500g); Apgar 4/8 (1/5 min) requiring NRP resuscitation; cord pH 7.18 (mild acidemia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neonatal outcome assessment. Apgar score guides initial resuscitation. Cord ABG quantifies intrapartum stress. NRP per standard protocol. NICU evaluation if concerns; rooming with mother if stable.

opts.text: IDM HYPOGLYCEMIA — baby BG 38 mg/dL (low for term newborn); fetal hyperinsulinism from maternal hyperglycemia → reactive hypoglycemia at delivery; feed immediately + recheck q30 min; D10W IV if <40 or symptomatic
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — IDM/IGDM complication. Hypoglycemia is the most common neonatal complication of maternal diabetes. Frequent feeding (q2–3h) prevents recurrence. D10W IV for symptomatic or persistent. BG monitoring per institutional protocol.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION of shoulder dystocia: times of head delivery, body delivery (head-to-body interval), maneuvers in order with times, persons present, estimated force, fetal heart rate, maternal VS, EBL, placenta, neonatal assessment — medico-legal critical
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — medico-legal protection + quality assessment. Standardized delivery note for shoulder dystocia. Each element matters. Required for any shoulder dystocia regardless of outcome.

opts.text: Maternal postpartum PPH RISK elevated: macrosomic infant + uterine overdistention + multiple maneuvers + prolonged 2nd stage; oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR running prophylactically; close monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — anticipated complication. PPH prophylaxis with oxytocin. Q15-min fundus + lochia + VS assessment x 4 hr. Uterotonic ladder if PPH develops (methergine OK in this patient with no HTN; carboprost if no asthma; misoprostol if both contraindications).

opts.text: Family + father support; father witnessed emergency delivery (PTSD-like symptoms common after birth trauma); postpartum depression screening at 2 + 6 wk visits; mental health resources offered
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial dimension. Partners witnessing emergency deliveries face significant psychological stress. Higher PPD/PTSD risk in both mother + partner after birth trauma + neonatal complications. Routine screening + mental health resources.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Estimated blood loss 450 mL (within normal range for vaginal delivery)
opts.rat: Reassuring — within normal range (<500 mL for vaginal delivery; <1000 mL for cesarean). Continue PPH monitoring given risk factors, but no current PPH.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / obstetric principles.

opts.text: SHOULDER DYSTOCIA = anterior shoulder impacted behind PUBIC SYMPHYSIS after delivery of head; obstetric emergency requiring time-sensitive maneuvers; TURTLE SIGN (head retracted) + failure of restitution + failure of anterior shoulder descent = recognition triad
opts.rat: CORRECT — disease definition. Anterior shoulder is the typical impacted shoulder. Head-to-body interval >60 sec = increasing concern; >5 min = significant fetal asphyxia risk. Standardized HELPERR mnemonic for management.

opts.text: HELPERR MNEMONIC: H (Help — call for help), E (Episiotomy — consider; doesn't relieve bony obstruction but creates room for internal maneuvers), L (Legs — McRoberts maneuver hyperflexion of thighs onto abdomen; ~40% success alone), P (suprapubic Pressure — palm above symphysis with downward + lateral force; NOT FUNDAL), E (Enter — internal rotation maneuvers Rubin + Wood's screw + reverse Wood's), R (Remove posterior arm), R (Roll — Gaskin all-fours position)
opts.rat: CORRECT — standardized algorithm. Each maneuver attempted ~30 sec before next. Order may vary based on situation/provider preference (some prefer Gaskin earlier in nulliparous patients). Documentation of all maneuvers + times essential.

opts.text: ⚠️ AVOID FUNDAL PRESSURE during shoulder dystocia — WORSENS impaction (pushes anterior shoulder more firmly against pubic symphysis), can cause UTERINE RUPTURE, worsens brachial plexus stretch injury; SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (palm above pubic symphysis, downward + lateral force) is the CORRECT technique to dislodge anterior shoulder
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield safety principle. Fundal vs suprapubic confusion is dangerous. All L&D staff must know the distinction. Document if fundal pressure inadvertently applied. The names sound similar but locations + effects are opposite.

opts.text: RISK FACTORS for shoulder dystocia: MACROSOMIA (especially >4500g), MATERNAL DIABETES (gestational or pre-existing; 3–7× risk via fetal hyperinsulinism causing abnormal shoulder-to-head proportion), PRIOR shoulder dystocia (10–25% recurrence), POSTTERM (>42 wk), prolonged 2nd stage, instrumental vaginal delivery, maternal obesity, advanced maternal age; HOWEVER most cases occur in patients WITHOUT identifiable risk factors
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk stratification + preparedness. Risk factors guide planning (consider elective cesarean for predicted fetal weight >5000g without DM or >4500g with DM per ACOG); however preparedness for all deliveries is essential because most occur in low-risk patients. Practice drills + simulation for L&D teams.

opts.text: ERB'S PALSY = upper brachial plexus (C5–C6) injury from EXCESSIVE LATERAL NECK TRACTION during shoulder dystocia delivery; classic 'WAITER'S TIP' position (arm adducted, internally rotated, elbow extended, wrist flexed); ABSENT MORO on affected side; intact grasp (C8–T1 spared); PROGNOSIS: 80–90% spontaneous recovery in 3–6 months with conservative management; persistent deficit at 3–6 months → microsurgical nerve repair consideration
opts.rat: CORRECT — common neonatal complication. Distinguished from KLUMPKE'S PALSY (lower brachial C8–T1 — 'CLAW HAND' + Horner's syndrome if T1 sympathetic chain involved). Conservative management is mainstay. Family education on positioning + ROM + expected recovery.

opts.text: INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER (IDM) / Infant of Gestational Diabetic (IGDM) RISKS: HYPOGLYCEMIA (fetal hyperinsulinism + abrupt loss of glucose supply at delivery), MACROSOMIA (insulin is growth factor in utero), respiratory distress (delayed lung maturity), polycythemia (chronic intrauterine hypoxia → erythropoietin → more RBCs), hypocalcemia, hyperbilirubinemia (more RBC breakdown), CONGENITAL ANOMALIES (more in pre-existing DM than GDM)
opts.rat: CORRECT — IDM/IGDM complications. Hypoglycemia is most common + immediately concerning. Feed immediately + frequent + BG monitoring. Polycythemia (Hct >65%) considered; rare to need partial exchange transfusion. Hyperbilirubinemia per AAP nomogram.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION of shoulder dystocia: time of HEAD delivery, time of BODY delivery (head-to-body interval), ALL MANEUVERS performed IN ORDER with TIMES, PERSONS present (named), estimated FORCE applied, FETAL HEART RATE pattern, MATERNAL VITAL SIGNS, ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS, NEONATAL ASSESSMENT including Apgars + brachial plexus exam + fracture assessment + initial resuscitation per NRP
opts.rat: CORRECT — medico-legal critical. Standardized delivery note for any shoulder dystocia regardless of outcome. Documentation protects providers + supports quality assessment + informs future care. Required by ACOG + institutional policy.

opts.text: MATERNAL COMPLICATIONS of shoulder dystocia: POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (uterine atony from overdistended uterus + multiple maneuvers; trauma from extended perineal/cervical lacerations), UTERINE RUPTURE (rare; risk with fundal pressure), BLADDER/URETHRAL trauma, PERIPARTUM PSYCHOLOGICAL TRAUMA
opts.rat: CORRECT — maternal-side considerations. PPH risk elevated; prophylactic oxytocin; close monitoring. Perineal/cervical inspection + repair. Psychological support essential. Postpartum follow-up + PPD/PTSD screening.

opts.text: Apply FUNDAL PRESSURE forcefully during shoulder dystocia to push the baby out
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Fundal pressure WORSENS impaction (pushes anterior shoulder more firmly against pubic symphysis), can cause UTERINE RUPTURE, worsens brachial plexus stretch injury. SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (palm above pubic symphysis, downward + lateral) is the correct technique. Critical safety distinction.

opts.text: Apply heavy traction on the fetal head to deliver the body during shoulder dystocia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Excessive lateral neck traction during shoulder dystocia is the primary cause of BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY (Erb's palsy, Klumpke's palsy). Gentle controlled traction only; rely on HELPERR maneuvers to relieve impaction first, then deliver with normal traction. Document estimated force applied for medico-legal protection.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Neonatal Asphyxia / Hypoxic Injury r/t shoulder dystocia with prolonged head-to-body interval; time-sensitive maneuvers
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST (during dystocia). HELPERR maneuvers performed efficiently (2 min head-to-body). Cord pH 7.18 mild acidemia, recovered by 5 min. NRP per standard protocol. Continued neonatal monitoring for HIE signs.

opts.text: Brachial Plexus Injury (Erb's Palsy) r/t shoulder dystocia delivery; lifelong functional implications if severe; conservative management + PT + close follow-up
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Erb's palsy confirmed on neonatal exam. 80–90% spontaneous recovery in 3–6 months with conservative management. Orthopedic + neurology + PT consultations. Parental education on positioning + ROM + expectations. Reassess at 1 month.

opts.text: Risk for Postpartum Hemorrhage r/t macrosomic infant + uterine overdistention + multiple maneuvers + 2nd stage; close monitoring + uterotonic prophylaxis
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. PPH prophylaxis with oxytocin. Q15-min fundus + lochia + VS x 4 hr. Uterotonic ladder if PPH develops. Methergine OK (no HTN); carboprost (no asthma); misoprostol if needed. TXA within 3 hr of bleeding.

opts.text: IDM Hypoglycemia r/t fetal hyperinsulinism + abrupt loss of glucose supply at delivery; frequent feeding + BG monitoring + D10W if symptomatic
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. BG q30 min initially → q3–6h x 24 hr. Frequent breastfeeding (q2–3h). D10W IV for symptomatic or BG <40. Most resolve over 24 hr with adequate feeding.

opts.text: Family Distress / Birth Trauma Sequelae r/t witnessing emergency delivery + neonatal complications; PPD/PTSD risk; screening + mental health resources
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Father witnessed emergency delivery. Mother experienced uncertainty + injury to baby. Postpartum depression + PTSD screening at 2 + 6 wk visits. Mental health resources offered.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: HELPERR sequence: H (Help — call for help), E (Episiotomy — consider), L (Legs — McRoberts maneuver), P (suprapubic Pressure — NOT FUNDAL), E (Enter — Rubin, Wood's screw), R (Remove posterior arm), R (Roll — Gaskin all-fours)
opts.rat: Standardized shoulder dystocia management. Each maneuver ~30 sec before next. Total <5 min ideally. Documentation of all maneuvers + times essential.

opts.text: FUNDAL PRESSURE forcefully to push the baby out during shoulder dystocia
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Worsens impaction, can cause uterine rupture, worsens brachial plexus stretch. SUPRAPUBIC PRESSURE (palm above pubic symphysis, downward + lateral) is the correct technique. Critical safety distinction.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION of shoulder dystocia: times of head + body delivery, maneuvers in order with times, persons present, estimated force, fetal heart rate, maternal VS, EBL, neonatal assessment including Apgars + brachial plexus exam + fracture assessment
opts.rat: Medico-legal critical + quality assessment + supports future care planning. Required by ACOG + institutional policy. Standardized delivery note for any shoulder dystocia.

opts.text: ERB'S PALSY management: immobilize R arm in functional position; gentle passive ROM only; orthopedic + neurology + PT consultation; conservative management 3–6 months; reassess at 1 month for nerve repair consideration if no improvement
opts.rat: Standard Erb's palsy management. 80–90% spontaneous recovery in 3–6 months. Conservative care is mainstay. Surgical referral if no improvement at 1 month. Parental education + expectations.

opts.text: CLAVICLE FRACTURE management: gentle handling + immobilization in functional position (sleeve pinned to clothes); X-ray confirms; usually heals without specific intervention beyond gentle handling
opts.rat: Standard clavicle fracture management. Common with macrosomia + shoulder dystocia. Heals well without surgical intervention. Pinning sleeve immobilizes. Reassess at 2 weeks (callus expected).

opts.text: Maternal PPH prophylaxis with oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR continuous; close monitoring fundus + lochia + VS q15 min x 4 hr; uterotonic ladder if PPH develops
opts.rat: PPH risk elevated due to macrosomia + uterine overdistention + multiple maneuvers. Standard oxytocin prophylaxis. Methergine OK (no HTN); carboprost (no asthma); misoprostol if needed. TXA within 3 hr of bleeding.

opts.text: IDM hypoglycemia management: BG q30 min until stable >40 then q3–6h x 24 hr; FREQUENT breastfeeding (q2–3h); D10W IV if BG <40 OR symptomatic
opts.rat: Standard IDM hypoglycemia protocol. Frequent feeding prevents recurrence. D10W via PIV for symptomatic or persistent. Most resolve over 24 hr with adequate feeding.

opts.text: AGGRESSIVE active ROM exercises of R arm to break up adhesions and restore function
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. GENTLE PASSIVE ROM only initially for Erb's palsy — aggressive movement can worsen injury or impede natural recovery. PT initiated as part of conservative management. After acute period, controlled exercises per PT guidance.

opts.text: FAMILY-CENTERED CARE: father at bedside; chaplain; social work; postpartum depression + PTSD screening at 2 + 6 wk; mental health resources offered (partner support also)
opts.rat: Standard postpartum + family support. Birth trauma + neonatal complications increase PPD/PTSD risk in both mother + partner. Routine screening + resources.

opts.text: Future pregnancy counseling at 6-wk visit: recurrence risk 10–25%; pre-pregnancy weight + diabetes optimization; planned delivery approach (induction at 39–40 wk if predicted macrosomic; elective cesarean for fetal weight >5000g without DM or >4500g with DM per ACOG)
opts.rat: Standard future pregnancy counseling after shoulder dystocia. Modifiable factors (weight, diabetes control). Shared decision-making for delivery approach. Document for future provider reference.

opts.text: PROMETHAZINE 12.5 mg IV for maternal nausea postpartum
opts.rat: Acceptable in adult patients (different from case-074 peds where promethazine has FDA black-box warning in <2 yr). Maternal nausea common postpartum; promethazine acceptable in adult, though ondansetron also commonly used + safer profile.

opts.text: Therapeutic hypothermia (cooling) for neonate within 6 hr of birth as preventive measure given the shoulder dystocia
opts.rat: Not preventive. Therapeutic hypothermia is for confirmed MODERATE-SEVERE HIE (hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy) — significant encephalopathy + seizures + abnormal tone + cord pH <7.0 or BE <-12. This infant had transient mild acidemia (cord pH 7.18) with recovery to Apgar 8 at 5 min — not hypothermia candidate. Reserve cooling for true HIE cases.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: RECOGNIZE turtle sign + CALL FOR HELP immediately (H of HELPERR); document time of head delivery; notify additional L&D staff + OB + anesthesia + NICU; continuous fetal monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Recognition + early activation of team. Time of head delivery starts the clock. Documentation begins. All hands needed for HELPERR maneuvers + neonatal resuscitation.

opts.text: Execute HELPERR maneuvers in standard order: L-Legs/McRoberts, P-suprapubic Pressure (NOT FUNDAL), E-Enter (Rubin + Wood's screw), R-Remove posterior arm, R-Roll if needed; episiotomy considered for room for internal maneuvers
opts.rat: #2 — Standardized maneuver sequence. Each ~30 sec before next. McRoberts first (40% success alone). Suprapubic pressure (NOT fundal). Internal maneuvers if external insufficient. Document each + times.

opts.text: NEONATAL CARE after delivery: NRP resuscitation per Apgar; cord ABG; comprehensive neonatal exam including brachial plexus exam + clavicle assessment + Apgars; BG (IDM); transition to mother for skin-to-skin if stable
opts.rat: #3 — Neonatal care. NRP standard protocol. Comprehensive assessment for shoulder dystocia complications (brachial plexus, clavicle). IDM-specific care (BG monitoring + feeding). Skin-to-skin transition if stable.

opts.text: MATERNAL POSTPARTUM CARE: oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR continuous for PPH prophylaxis; q15-min assessment fundus + lochia + perineum + VS x 4 hr; ibuprofen for pain; perineum ice + sitz baths after 24 hr; voiding within 6 hr
opts.rat: #4 — Maternal care + PPH prevention. Standard immediate postpartum care + heightened monitoring given PPH risk factors. Perineum care. Bladder management.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION (medico-legal): times of head delivery, body delivery, maneuvers in order with times, persons present, force, fetal heart rate, maternal VS, EBL, neonatal assessment; ORTHO + NEURO + PT consultations for Erb's palsy + clavicle fracture; family-centered care + birth trauma support + future pregnancy counseling at 6-wk
opts.rat: #5 — Documentation + sustained care + family. Documentation is medico-legal critical. Multidisciplinary consultations for neonatal complications. Family-centered care + mental health support. Discharge planning + outpatient follow-up structure.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Body delivered at 1432:18 with HELPERR maneuvers (2 min head-to-body); Apgar 4/8 (1/5 min) after NRP; transitioning to mother for skin-to-skin once stable; rooming with mother for monitoring
opts.rat: Successful shoulder dystocia management with timely maneuvers + adequate NRP. Apgar recovery to 8 at 5 min reassuring. Continued monitoring for HIE signs (rare with brief acidemia); BG monitoring (IDM); Erb's palsy + clavicle fracture management.

opts.text: Discharge day 2: mother stable, EBL 450 mL (normal), perineum healing, breastfeeding well, lactation support + outpatient lactation referral; baby BG stable on feedings, gaining weight; Erb's palsy progressing with PT, R clavicle healing with callus formation; family attended Erb's palsy education + PT instruction
opts.rat: Successful postpartum recovery. Multidisciplinary follow-up scheduled (pediatrics, orthopedics, neurology, PT, lactation). Parental education + advocacy. Most Erb's palsies resolve in 3–6 months.

opts.text: 6-week postpartum visit: mother recovered well, depressed mood mild + improving with counseling + PPD support group, no recurrent fertility plan discussed yet; baby Erb's palsy with R arm spontaneous movement returning (improving); R clavicle fully healed; growing well; pediatric + neuro + PT continuing follow-up
opts.rat: Long-term recovery. PPD addressed with support + counseling. Erb's palsy spontaneously improving as expected (most resolve in 3–6 months). Future pregnancy counseling completed. Comprehensive multidisciplinary follow-up structure.

opts.text: Hour 4 postpartum: mother develops PPH with EBL 1500 mL from uterine atony (overdistended uterus from macrosomia); fundus boggy despite oxytocin; methergine 0.2 mg IM given (no HTN — OK for this patient); bimanual fundal massage; TXA 1 g IV; fundus firms
opts.rat: Expected complication given risk factors. PPH from atony in overdistended uterus. Uterotonic ladder (methergine OK no HTN; carboprost no asthma; misoprostol if needed). Bimanual massage. TXA within 3 hr. MTP if hemodynamic compromise.

opts.text: Day 1: baby develops poor feeding, lethargy, jitteriness; BG 28 mg/dL (worsening hypoglycemia despite frequent feeding); D10W IV started; recheck in 30 min; bilirubin 14 (elevated; phototherapy started); NICU admission for closer monitoring
opts.rat: Worsening IDM complications. D10W IV for symptomatic hypoglycemia. Phototherapy for hyperbilirubinemia per AAP nomogram. NICU for closer monitoring + IV support. Resolves with continued feeding + IV support + phototherapy.

opts.text: 1-month outpatient neurology + PT visit: minimal recovery in R arm function (still in 'waiter's tip' position, absent Moro persists); MRI brachial plexus reveals neurotmesis (severe injury); referral to peds plastic/microsurgery for nerve repair consideration
opts.rat: Persistent severe Erb's palsy concerning for severe nerve injury. Microsurgical nerve repair consideration (typically at 3 months if no recovery). PT continues. Parental education on expectations + functional adaptations. Most still recover substantial function with intervention.

opts.text: Postpartum debriefing session at 1 week: team discusses what worked well + identifies improvement opportunities; not punitive — culture of learning; reinforces team training for future shoulder dystocia events
opts.rat: Post-event debriefing is best practice. Identifies improvements without blame (Just Culture). Reinforces team training. Simulation can be scheduled to practice. Improves outcomes for future patients.

opts.text: Father presents to PCP at 4 weeks with insomnia, intrusive memories of delivery, hypervigilance — partner PTSD diagnosed; referred to mental health for therapy + support
opts.rat: Partner PTSD after witnessing birth trauma is real + underrecognized. Validate symptoms; refer to mental health (cognitive behavioral therapy, EMDR effective); peer support; family therapy if affecting relationship dynamics. Long-term recovery with appropriate care.

opts.text: Mother postpartum at 6-week visit: still struggling with intrusive memories of delivery + guilt about baby's Erb's palsy; Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score 14 (positive for likely PPD); referred to mental health + support group
opts.rat: Expected emotional response after birth trauma + neonatal complications. EPDS positive score → mental health referral. Therapy + possible SSRI + support group + partner therapy. Most respond well with appropriate care. Reassure mother.

opts.text: Baby at 6-month follow-up: full functional recovery of R arm with normal grasp, normal Moro, intact ROM; R clavicle fully healed with callus formation; growing well; no Erb's palsy residual; pediatric + orthopedic + neurology + PT discharged
opts.rat: Best-case outcome for Erb's palsy + clavicle fracture (occurs in 80–90% of Erb's palsies with conservative management). Comprehensive recovery. Continued routine pediatric care. Family education + counseling complete.
